prompt:
The status-page renderer strikes again. Last night's incident summary showed `Ops &amp; Dev` in a tooltip while the SAME value rendered fine in the paragraph below it, and a "more details" link went dead because somebody's next= parameter contained an unencoded ampersand. Root cause is obvious: we push every value through one global escape helper no matter where in the HTML it lands. I want a small template layer that understands WHERE each placeholder sits and escapes accordingly: ctxtmpl.py, stdlib only.

Scope guard so nobody gold-plates this: placeholders are `{{ name }}` (optional inner whitespace, name = [A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*), plain substitution only — no loops, no conditionals, no dotted paths, no filters. We already have a big engine for layout; this layer exists to get the escaping right per sink.

Exports:
- render(source, values) -> str. Values are coerced with str(). A placeholder whose name is missing from values raises KeyError. STATIC template text is the author's own HTML and passes through completely untouched — only substituted values get escaped.
- placeholder_contexts(source) -> list of (name, context) tuples, one per placeholder occurrence, in template order. Context is one of "text", "attr", "url-full", "url-part". This powers a lint step in CI, so it must run the same context scan render uses.
- TemplateSyntaxError and TemplateContextError (the latter with .name and .where attributes).

Context is INFERRED from the template's own HTML around the placeholder:
1. "text" — between tags. Escape the value: & -> &, < -> <, > -> >. Quotes stay as-is in text.
2. "attr" — inside a quoted attribute value (double OR single quotes both count) of any attribute that is not href/src. Escape & < > plus \" -> " and ' -> '.
3. "url-full" — the placeholder starts immediately after the opening quote of an href or src value (it IS the URL). Check the scheme of str(value).strip() via urllib.parse.urlsplit: empty scheme (relative), http, https, or mailto (compare lowercased) means the value is inserted as given, escaped like an attribute. Any other scheme — ftp:, data:, app:, whatever the preview can't open — and the attribute value renders as the literal single character #. If urlsplit refuses the value, treat that as unsupported too.
4. "url-part" — anywhere else inside an href/src value (query parameter, path segment, second placeholder after a url-full one...). Percent-encode the whole substituted value with urllib.parse.quote(str(value), safe="") — spaces become %20, & becomes %26, non-ascii encodes as its utf-8 bytes.
href/src count as URL attributes on EVERY tag (a script tag's src included).

Places a placeholder cannot go — TemplateContextError at scan time, with .where set to: "tag" (inside a tag but not in a quoted value: tag name or attribute name position), "unquoted-attr" (attribute value without quotes), "script-style" (anywhere inside a script or style element body — tag matching is case-insensitive, and text after the closing tag is normal text again), "comment" (inside <!-- -->). The scan runs before any value lookup, so these fire even when values is empty — no KeyError first.

Malformed placeholders — `{{` never closed, empty name, `{{ user.name }}` (dotted), a name starting with a digit — raise TemplateSyntaxError.

Watch the scanner details: a quoted attribute value may contain > without ending the tag, and both quote styles nest each other's quote character fine. The acceptance contract is test_ctxtmpl.py — python3 test_ctxtmpl.py green, test file read-only, stdlib only, deterministic.
